text: to leave our terrors behind in those low peaks. The wagon clattered on bent wheels. Five miles. The sun a white blur in the center of everything and yet the dry ache of the desert could not do its work to our bones in such a length. We rode at an easy clip and the worry dropped away from our faces. Soro and William, side by side, discussed the shapes and colors of the whores they would buy in the city, while Huedro called ahead to remind them they had plump young wives keeping their beds warm. Lupe the Older, he who was now mere Lupe, rode next to me and we each sat in the quiet of our own minds. But far too early the land drained of its heat and the horizon to the west bled an array of inks. We squinted ahead through the whirling grit and saw that the lines of El Porvenir had changed. Something huge and rounded, like a mountain peak made blunt, pushed up above the buildings. “Blood man,” Huedro called as Julo’s horse drew near, “what does this mean?” We drank from canteens and watered the horses from our hats. The blood man stared across those miles at the city and his eye socket seemed to dilate. After a minute he answered. “It means you wait until dawn, whatever dawn may be. Mark me now. I’m the only of us who must go into that place.” He kicked his horse forward and the hide he wore flapped at his back like a lost flag. The sun slipped meekly away and a pure black began to clog each pore of the sky save for a dense cluster of stars near the bowl’s center. I pictured a plughole opened and the celestial wash cluttered at its mouth. Under this unnatural constellation we debated each to himself. I had never heard such profound quiet. It seemed to eat the creak of leather and the snorts of the animals. “Do we ride on?” William asked. He looked like snow in the darkness against the chestnut of his mount. “Don’t much like the thought of bedding down here.” “Gather a fire,” I answered, for the last light had all but fled. There was little material for it, so we collected the dryer, topmost bison hides. Soon a greasy smoke clouded our meager camp, the stench from the burning skins heavy and close. The horses clomped off to the edge of the firelight. A supper of dried meat was passed around. We wondered aloud if the blood man hadn’t deceived us and circled back into the mountains. For if fear had mastered that strange man then what hope had we from this unknown? My eyes never strayed long from those stars converged in their basin. Whether the remainder of that mute endless sky was empty of all things or muddied with approaching menace, none could say. The whole of it quivered with meaning and we shrank to nothing more than dust in its lidless eye. Lupe took the first watch while the rest of us wrapped ourselves in hides and curled up in that void, lashed together once more. The city had fallen into it for all we knew. Only the same blackness lay in its direction and even that great hump was obscured. Huedro was the first. I’d taken second watch and I saw him open his eyes and with his teeth pull apart the rope binding his wrist to Sorrén’s. He stood and let the firelight flicker across his face for a moment before turning away. “Coraza?” he whispered toward the dark and then walked off. It was the name of his mother who was said to be passing fair. “Huedro!” I hissed his name again and again but he was lost, absorbed into a place I would not go. After Soro cut his bonds and wandered off into the same dark, I roused the others, cursing my frail self. There was no more sleep. # Dawn came and in it El Porvenir gave the illusion of having crawled through the small hours toward us. The earthen dome seemed to gather the city into itself, although its apex had now broken or caved in from some structural flaw. The horses hadn’t moved in the night. We rode out trailing the two riderless animals behind us. The sky had emptied of all life and the hardpan was dotted with black carcasses of fallen ruecrows and vultures. We passed inside the low walls of the city onto streets shrouded in a silent pall. A hearsewagon sat full of the dead in front of an unmarked adobe building. As we watched an old white woman crept from beneath the canvas cover. She sat in the clay street and slowly pulled out her eyes on their stalks. More bodies shuffled within the wagon, the canvas bulging, and we nudged our horses on. Any taste for good whiskey had withered in our guts. An empty cantina slid by and there civilization ended, for soon we came to the immense mound. Twenty or more feet high, the thing was surely crafted upon some god’s potter wheel, still glistening as though newly sculpted. From within a wide hole near its base issued a throng of voices in murmurs and halting laughter, unhindered by the collapse of the mound’s roof. The air carried a moist tang. Embedded in the soft mud of the structure were the naked waists and legs of figures twitching like worms after a hard rain. The blood man’s bare horse lay dead in the gray light. “What now?” William said. “Looks like the governor ain’t in.” “The others could be in there,” I said, but I was thinking of Isa, for I’d picked her voice out among the soft din. “And I need gold to pay for any years my wife has left to her.” “Well, I ain’t going in that hole,” Sorrén said even as he sat gazing into
